turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has our opinion this morning, case 17-778, Quarles v. United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The Armed Career Criminal Act is a federal law that mandates a minimum 15-year prison sentence for a felon who unlawfully possesses a firearm and has three prior convictions for a violent felony. The Act defines 'violent felony' to include 'burglary.' Under this Court's 1990 decision in Taylor v. United States, the generic statutory term 'burglary' means unlawful or unprivileged entry into, or remaining in, a building or structure, with intent to commit a crime. The question in this case is how to define remaining-in burglary under the Armed Career Criminal Act in our precedent in Taylor. Remaining-in burglary cover situations where a person enters a building lawfully but stays unlawfully. For example, a person who enters a store lawfully but then remains in the store after closing time without permission and then intentionally steals something from the store. In this case, Jamar Quarles pled guilty to the federal crime of being a felon in possession of a firearm. Quarles had three prior convictions that qualified as violent felonies under the Armed Career Criminal Act, thereby triggering a mandatory minimum 15-year prison sentence. In this court, Quarles argues that one of his three prior convictions, his 2002 Michigan conviction for third-degree home invasion, swept too broadly to constitute remaining in burglary under the Armed Career Criminal Act. That Michigan law applies when a defendant forms the intent to commit a crime at any time while unlawfully present in a building. Quarles asserts that remaining in burglary for purposes of the Armed Career Criminal Act occurs only when a defendant has the intent to commit a crime at the exact moment when he or she first unlawfully remains in a building. The government argues for a broader definition. According to the Government, remaining-in burglary occurs when the defendant forms the intent to commit a crime at any time while unlawfully present in a building. Under the Government's theory, the Michigan law under which Quarles was convicted in 2002 would readily qualifies remaining in burglary for purposes of the Armed Career Criminal Act. We agree with the government's argument. The common understanding of remaining in as a continuous event means that burglary occurs for purposes of the Armed Career Criminal Act if the defendant forms the intent to commit a crime at any time during the continuous event of unlawfully remaining in a building. Quarles contends that to constitute a burglary under the Act, the intent to commit a crime must be contemporaneous with unlawful remaining. That is true. But the defendant's intent is contemporaneous with the unlawful remaining so long as the defendant forms the intent to commit a crime at any time while unlawfully remaining in the building. As the Court recognized in its 1990 decision in Taylor moreover, Congress singled out burglary because of burglary's inherent potential for harm to persons. Burglary is dangerous because it creates the possibility of a violent confrontation between the offender and an occupant. With respect to remaining in burglary, the possibility of a violent confrontation does not depend on the exact moment when the burglar forms the intent to commit a crime while unlawfully present in a building. Once an intruder is both unlawfully present inside a building and has the requisite intent to commit a crime, all of the reasons that led Congress to include burglary as a predicate offense fully apply. The dangers of remaining-in burglary are not tied to the esoteric question of precisely when the defendant forms the intent to commit a crime while inside a building. In addition, in interpreting remaining-in burglary as Quarles' advocates, would thwart the stated goals of the Armed Career Criminal Act. If we adopted Quarles' narrow interpretation, many State Burglary statutes would sweep more broadly than the Act's generic definition of burglary and those States' burglary statutes would therefore be eliminated as predicate offenses under the Armed Career Criminal Act. That result not only would defy commonsense, but also would defeat Congress' stated objective of imposing enhanced punishment on armed career criminals who have three prior convictions for burglary or other violent felonies. We should not lightly conclude that Congress enacted a self-defeating statute. In sum, remaining in burglary occurs under the Armed Career Criminal Act when the defendant forms the intent to commit a crime at anytime while unlawfully remaining in a building. For the Court's purposes here, the Michigan Statute under which Quarles was convicted in 2002, substantially corresponds to generic burglary under the Armed Career Criminal Act and therefore qualifies as a predicate offense under the Act. For those and other reasons, we affirm the judgment of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit. The opinion is unanimous. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion.
